title: Stepped Hitlists for Performance
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
Performance Guidelines for Component Logic and Implementation
text: Stepped Hitlists for Performance Uniface uses a stepped hitlist to improve performance. This mechanism ensures that a limited number of occurrences are fetched from the database, even though a large number of occurrences match the retrieve profile entered by the user. ProcScript instructions and hitlist functionality can affect performance. Step Size For some DBMSs, the size of the stepped hitlist can be set to values other than the default value of ten occurrences. Setting the size to a higher value can improve retrieval performance. Setting it to zero disables the stepped hitlist mechanism. retrieve The retrieve statement, eventually combined with /e or /o , activates the read statement. Any previously built hitlists of referenced entities on the active path are discarded. The retrieve/a instruction appends the occurrences that match a newly issued retrieve profile to the existing hitlist. It enables re-use of already retrieved data by one or more processes. The retrieve/x instruction retrieves an additional occurrence of an entity without discarding the current hitlist. If there is a partially completed stepped hitlist, the hitlist is completed before a new occurrence is retrieved. discard The discard instruction removes an occurrence from the hitlist and the Uniface form without physically deleting it from the database. This instruction can be used to release memory in large batch jobs, so that more transactions can be executed without loss of performance. findkey The findkey instruction checks whether the specified key values exist in the current component or in the database. The instruction does not validate anything and can perform better than the retrieve/o instruction. lookup The lookup instruction returns the number of occurrences that match the current profile. The instruction has the advantage of not performing any I/O operations, because the occurrences are not fetched from the database. Instead, the return status of this instruction indicates the result (found or not found). The retrieve instruction fetches the occurrence from the database and builds up a hitlist, and consequently performs more slowly. $hits Using the $hits function in a ProcScript instruction causes Uniface to complete the hitlist of all occurrences in the entity that match the retrieve profile. As a consequence, the performance benefits of using a stepped hitlist are lost. Using $hits can be time-consuming in situations where the number of hits is large. It is recommended that you use $hits only if absolutely necessary or if the number of hits is small. store/truncate and store/complete The store and store/truncate instructions preserve the existing hitlist and prevent any incomplete hitlists from being completed. The store/complete instruction completes the hitlist before the store process is executed, allowing users to periodically store changes without disrupting the list of occurrences they are working on. sort The sort instruction sorts the occurrences in the hitlist according to specified criteria. Usually a large number of data is involved in a sorting process. The sorting is done in real memory and perhaps in virtual memory, so this instruction can severely degrade performance. Preventing the Hitlist from Being Completed or Rebuilt Try to prevent referencing entities that cause existing hitlists to be completed and new ones to be rebuilt. Otherwise, the advantage of the stepped hitlist mechanism for the existing hitlist is lost. If possible, use existing hitlists in a single transaction without rebuilding them. For example, creating an instance using the Transaction property set to TRUE results in a new path, including an external data structure and stepped hitlist. This feature applies only to non-modal forms. Clearing Hitlists The hitlist mechanism is intended to reduce the time required to retrieve data. If Uniface needs a specific record for a specific entity, it first looks through the existing hitlists to see if the data is already available, so that it does not need to do a database retrieve. However, if there are many hitlists and these do not contain the required record, this mechanism can slow retrieval rather than speed it. To prevent this problem, you can use the clear/e ProcScript statement. For example, consider a case where ENT_A has a foreign key field referring to ENT_B, and ENT_B has a foreign key field referring to ENT_C. In the following ProcScript: This statement changes the foreign key field of ENT_B (referring to ENT_C) in its current record, so the current record of ENT_C is no longer valid. As a result, as soon as any field of ENT_C is accessed, a new retrieve must be done in order to update that field's value. This creates a new hitlist because the previous hitlist was based on a different primary key, resulting in as many hitlists as there are iterations through the inner while loop. The same is also true for the outer while loop. The current record of ENT_B becomes invalid, needing a new retrieve , resulting in a new hitlist. So, the number of hitlist Uniface is maintaining increases by the number of iterations through this outer loop as well. setocc "ENT_A", 1 while ($status >= 0) setocc "ENT_B", 1 while ($status >= 0) if (SOME_FIELD.ENT_C == "some value") 1 ; <= bad performance ; any code endif setocc "ENT_B", $curocc(ENT_B) + 1 2 endwhile setocc "ENT_A", $curocc(ENT_A) + 1 3 endwhile Copy To prevent this, you can insert clear/e prior to incrementing the occurrence. For example: setocc "ENT_A", 1 while ($status >= 0) setocc "ENT_B", 1 while ($status >= 0) if (SOME_FIELD.ENT_C == "some value") ; <= bad performance ; any code endif clear/e "ENT_B" ; clear hitlist setocc "ENT_B", $curocc(ENT_B) + 1 endwhile clear/e "ENT_A" ; clear hitlist setocc "ENT_A", $curocc(ENT_A) + 1 endwhile Copy Related Links retrieve Activate the read trigger for the first outermost entity and all related entities, or for a specific entity. $hits Return the number of occurrences in the hitlist. clear Clear data from the component or entity. discard Remove one or more occurrences from the component and the hitlist. findkey Check if the key value of an entity exists. lookup Count the number of occurrences that match the current profile. store Initiate validation and storage of all occurrences marked as modified. It activates the appropriate validation triggers as required, followed by the write and delete triggers. sort Sort the occurrences in the hitlist for the specified entity. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
